en.extract:
The striped owl (Asio clamator) is a medium-sized owl with large ear tufts and a brownish-white facial disk rimmed with black.  Its beak is black, and it has cinnamon-colored eyes. It has shorter, rounder wings than most of its close relatives. The upperparts are cinnamon with fine black vermiculation and heavy stripes. The underparts are pale tawny with dusky streaks. It is native to South America and parts of Central America.


Taxonomy
The striped owl was formally described by the French ornithologist Louis Pierre Vieillot in 1808 under the binomial name Bubo clamator. The specific epithet clamator is Latin meaning "shouter". The type locality is Cayenne in French Guiana. The striped owl was at one time placed in its own genus Rhinoptynx and was then transferred to the genus Pseudoscops. A molecular study that compared mitochondrial DNA sequences indicated that it should be placed in the genus Asio. This result was confirmed by a large molecular phylogenetic study of the owls published in 2019.


Description
The striped owl is a relatively large species with prominent tufts of elongated feathers on the crown resembling ears. It is 30–38 cm (12–15 in) long and weighs from 320 to 546 g (11.3 to 19.3 oz). Its head, back, wings and tail are brown with black stripes and small markings while its underparts are buff-coloured with heavy black streaking on the breast. The facial disk is pure white with a thin black border.


Distribution and habitat
The striped owl is native to much of South and Central America. Its range is not well known, perhaps because it is nocturnal and not easily seen, but it is known from Argentina, Belize, Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Costa Rica, Ecuador, El Salvador, French Guiana, Guatemala, Guyana, Honduras, Mexico, Nicaragua, Panama, Paraguay, Peru, Suriname, Trinidad and Tobago, Uruguay and Venezuela. It uses a variety of habitats, including riparian woodlands, marshes, savannahs, grassy open areas, and tropical rainforests. It can be found from sea level to an altitude of 1,600 m (5,200 ft) and above.


Conservation status
The striped owl has a very large range and its population is believed to be stable. It faces no particular threats and is classified by the IUCN as least concern.


References


External links

Striped Owl videos on the Internet Bird Collection

| range_map = Asio clamator map.svg }} The '''striped owl''' ('''''Asio clamator''''') is a medium-sized owl with large ear tufts and a brownish-white facial disk rimmed with black. Its beak is black, and it has cinnamon-colored eyes. It has shorter, rounder wings than most of its close relatives. The upperparts are cinnamon with fine black vermiculation and heavy stripes. The underparts are pale tawny with dusky streaks. It is native to [[South America]] and parts of [[Central America]]. [[File:Striped Owl.jpg|200px|thumbnail|Striped owl]] ==Taxonomy== The striped owl was [[species description|formally described]] by the French ornithologist [[Louis Pierre Vieillot]] in 1808 under the [[binomial name]] ''Bubo clamator''.{{ cite book | last=Vieillot | first=Louis Pierre | author-link=Louis Pierre Vieillot | year=1807 | title=Histoire naturelle des oiseaux de l'Amérique Septentrionale: contenant un grand nombre d'espèces décrites ou figurées pour la première fois | volume=1 | language=French | location=Paris | publisher=Chez Desray | page=52, Plate 20 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47004141 }} Although the title page bears the year 1807, the volume was not publish until 1808. This result was confirmed by a large [[molecular phylogenetic]] study of the owls published in 2019.{{Cite journal | last1=Salter | first1=J.F. | last2=Oliveros | first2=C.H. | last3=Hosner | first3=P.A. | last4=Manthey | first4=J.D. | last5=Robbins | first5=M.B. | last6=Moyle | first6=R.G. | last7=Brumfield | first7=R.T. | last8=Faircloth | first8=B.C. | date=2019 | title=Extensive paraphyly in the typical owl family (Strigidae) | journal=The Auk | volume=137 | issue=ukz070 | doi=10.1093/auk/ukz070| doi-access=free | hdl=2346/93048 | hdl-access=free }}{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=January 2021 | title=Owls | work=IOC World Bird List Version 11.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/owls/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=17 May 2021 }} ==Description== The striped owl is a relatively large species with prominent tufts of elongated feathers on the crown resembling ears. It is {{convert|30|-|38|cm|in|abbr=on}} long and weighs from {{convert|320|to|546|g|oz|abbr=on}}.[http://www.owlpages.com/owls.php?genus=Asio&species=clamator] Owl pages (2011). Its head, back, wings and tail are brown with black stripes and small markings while its underparts are buff-coloured with heavy black streaking on the breast. The facial disk is pure white with a thin black border.{{cite web |url=https://neotropical.birds.cornell.edu/portal/species/identification?p_p_spp=36400 |title=''Pseudoscops clamator'': Striped Owl |author=Thurber, Walter A. |author2=Rebecca Lohnes |author3=Thomas S. Schulenberg |name-list-style=amp |year=2009 |work=Neotropical Birds Online |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology |access-date=2013-12-22}} ==Distribution and habitat== The striped owl is native to much of South and Central America. Its range is not well known, perhaps because it is nocturnal and not easily seen, but it is known from Argentina, Belize, Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Costa Rica, Ecuador, El Salvador, French Guiana, Guatemala, Guyana, Honduras, Mexico, Nicaragua, Panama, Paraguay, Peru, Suriname, Trinidad and Tobago, Uruguay and Venezuela. It uses a variety of habitats, including riparian woodlands, marshes, savannahs, grassy open areas, and [[tropical rainforest]]s. It can be found from sea level to an altitude of {{convert|1600|m|abbr=on}} and above. ==Conservation status== The striped owl has a very large range and its population is believed to be stable.
